text: Hi, and welcome to This is My Architecture.
I'm Andy, and I'm here with Anand from Flutura.
Hi, Anand.
Hi, Andy.
So, Anand, I see an architecture on here, but
I also see some things are going on.
But before we get into this, if you wouldn't mind,
can you tell me about Flutura?
Sure.
Flutura, we are an industrial AI and IoT company.
We provide digital assistance to our customers that
helps them improve operational efficiency and asset uptime.
Excellent.
So, if you can, can you give me an idea here?
So you have an oral, Derek.
How does that relate back to the rest of this architecture?
Sure.
So we had a customer who was facing production
deferment in the gas fields.
So they were looking for an ML-based solution that can
predict anomalies in the gas field before they happen.
So we took the data from there, and we used this architecture
to implement that ML models.
Then how do you get the data from the actual device
into your architecture here?
Sure.
Sure.
So the data from the control system and edge devices come
to our VPC that's in the cloud through MQTT or MQTT
connectors.
And then the data is pushed by those connectors on the Kafka topic,
which is we are using MSK to manage the Kafka topics.
Once the data is in the MSK cluster, the Lambda triggers the Spark job running on the EMR cluster.
That Lambda is set up to trigger every minute.
It's on top of a minute.
It runs that.
The Spark job is our data parser, which reads the data from the Kafka topic,
cleans the data, perform data validation operations,
and then write to our OpenTSDB, which is our time series database.
That's open source.
And that sits on top of edge base.
Right?
And we run the edge base as well on the same EMR cluster.
So the OpenTSDB writes to the cluster.
Sorry, the edge base.
And then that data gets written back to the S3 buckets.
So this is your sanitized data then that's been through the processing jobs
from your cluster?
Correct.
Correct.
So I see, Ray, EMR is really at the center of this here.
So why did you choose EMR?
Sure.
So EMR, we were looking for a platform that can run Spark and edge base,
since we use that heavily.
And EMR's kind of suited our needs very well because of the scalable architecture.
So during our peak needs, we can increase the computation power
without changing the storage.
And then during the normal hours, we bring that back to normal cluster size.
And as we are growing in the data, we are slowly increasing the storage
without changing the computation.
So that helps a lot for us.
Excellent.
So after you have your sanitized data, what happens next?
Sure.
So the parser that's after cleaning the data writing to the database,
it also publishes the data back to another topic on the Kafka.
From there, our data analytical engine runs that and reads the data
from there, next topic.
And that's evaluating the rules.
There are thousands of rules that gets evaluated in a minute.
And then the notification of that goes to the user as well.
And also, we have machine learning models that are running and executing
in the easy-to instances that reads the real-time data from MSK.
And then the historical data from OpenTSDB, which basically is data sitting in S3.
And the contextual information, which is the equipment information,
such as digital twin configuration, from the Postgres database that we have.
Using all this information, it predicts the values.
And this could be a set point prediction for equipment.
It could be a failure prediction or any prediction that we would like to build a model for.
Then these predictions are presented to the user through our web application
that's running on the easy-to instances.
And also, they can register for notification through SMS or emails.
And after that, with some of our customers, we also write the data back to the field.
That forms the closed loop.
So control system can accept the data and change the set points for operational efficiency
of the equipment.
So I'm just curious here then.
So for the amount of data that's moving in here, can you give us a sense of how much
that you're dealing with?
Yeah.
With one of our customer, we recorded the number of data points.
So we got about a billion records per day.
That's the throughput of the system that we noticed.
And then the rules that we were evaluating was over 50K rules in a minute.
Wow.
Thank you for sharing that story, Adnan.
My pleasure.
And thank you for watching This is My Architecture.
